topics.name: Ch 4: Decline of the Mughal Empire

content.question: 1) Differentiate between solids, liquids and gases on the basis of kinetic theory.
content.answer:
Property Solids Liquids Gases
Molecular Arrangement The molecules of a solid are very tightly packed. The molecules of a liquid are loosely packed. The molecules of a gas are far separated from each other.
Intermolecular Spacing The intermolecular spacing is the least in solids. The intermolecular spacing in liquids is more than that in solids but less than that in gases. The intermolecular spacing is the maximum in gases.
Intermolecular Force The intermolecular force is the strongest in solids. The intermolecular force in liquids is stronger than that in gases but weaker than that in solids. The intermolecular force is the weakest in gases.
Molecular Motion Molecules of a solid possess vibratory motion of very small amplitude. Molecules of a liquid possess random motion within the boundary of the liquid. Molecules of a gas possess random motion in the whole volume of the containing vessel.

content.question: 2) Differentiate between cohesive force and adhesive force with examples.
content.answer:
Type of Force Definition Examples
Cohesive Force The force of attraction between similar kinds of molecules.
  • Molecules of mercury remaining sticking to each other in a drop of mercury.
  • Molecules of iron sticking to each other in a piece of iron.
Adhesive Force The force of attraction between different kinds of molecules.
  • The force of attraction between paper and glue.
  • The force of attraction between paper and ink.
  • The force of attraction between cement and brick.

content.question: 9)Write one difference between gas and vapor.
content.answer:
Feature Gas Vapor
Definition A substance that exists naturally in the gaseous state at standard room temperature and pressure (e.g., Oxygen, Nitrogen). The gaseous state of a substance that is normally a solid or liquid at room temperature (e.g., Water vapor, Iodine vapor).
Thermodynamic State It is above its critical temperature. It cannot be liquefied by pressure alone, no matter how much pressure is applied. It is below its critical temperature. It can be condensed back into a liquid or solid simply by increasing the pressure.

Summary for an Exam/Test

If you need a quick, one-line difference for an assessment, you can write:

"A gas is a substance that exists naturally in the gaseous state at room temperature, whereas a vapor is the gaseous form of a substance that is normally a liquid or solid at room temperature."

content.content:
**Executive summary:** Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space; it exists in three main forms — solid, liquid and gas. The **molecule** is the smallest independent unit showing all properties of a substance. **Cohesion** (same substance) and **adhesion** (different substances) are the two intermolecular attractions; their strength depends on the nature of the molecules and the distance between them.

**Must know**
• **Matter** = has mass + occupies space. Examples: iron, water, air, plastic, wood, stone, oil, steam.
• Matter is composed of **elements**. One kind of element only → smallest unit = **atom**. Two or more elements combined → smallest unit = **molecule**.
• A molecule may consist of one or more atoms: a **water** molecule = 2 hydrogen atoms + 1 oxygen atom; a **common salt** molecule = 1 sodium atom + 1 chlorine atom.
• Molecules exert forces on one another — these **intermolecular forces** are responsible for various properties of a substance.
• Molecules are in a state of **continuous motion** and thus possess energy.
• **Force of cohesion (cohesive force)**: intermolecular attraction amongst molecules of the **same** substance.
• **Force of adhesion (adhesive force)**: attraction between molecules of **different** substances — it makes substances stick to surfaces (paint adhering to a wall or door).
• The **magnitude** of intermolecular force depends on (1) the **nature** of the molecules and (2) the **intermolecular distance**. It is **strongest in solids, weakest in gases**.

**Activity evidence (hammer test):** beat chalk, brick and iron with a hammer — chalk powders easily, brick breaks with difficulty, iron hardly breaks. Conclusion: cohesion exists in all, but **chalk < brick << iron**.
• Tearing a sheet of paper = doing work **against the force of cohesion** between paper molecules.
content.explanation:
**📊 Chart — Cohesion vs Adhesion**
| Criterion | Cohesion | Adhesion |
|---|---|---|
| Acts between | Molecules of the **same** substance | Molecules of **different** substances |
| Examples | Water drops merging; tearing paper needs work against it | Paint sticking to a wall; glue–paper; ink–paper |
| Strength comparison | Chalk (weak) < brick < iron (very strong) | Decides how well liquids wet surfaces |
| Exam trap | 'Adhesion acts in the same substance' = FALSE | Painting a wall uses adhesion, not cohesion |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Define matter and give four examples.
• How many atoms of which elements make one water molecule? One salt molecule?
• Which force do you work against while tearing paper?
• On what two factors does the magnitude of intermolecular force depend?
• In which state is intermolecular force strongest and in which weakest?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Atom = smallest unit of an ELEMENT; molecule = smallest unit of MATTER that exists independently with all its properties.
• The hammer activity compares COHESION, not hardness for its own sake.
content.examTip: Cohesion = same substance, adhesion = different substances — and remember the two factors controlling their magnitude: nature of molecules and intermolecular distance.

content.subtopic: 2. The Three States of Matter — Molecular Arrangement
content.content:
**Executive summary:** The properties of solids, liquids and gases follow from three molecular quantities: packing (intermolecular space), force strength, and type of motion. Solids: vibration only → fixed shape. Liquids: molecules slide within the liquid → fixed volume, free surface, no fixed shape. Gases: independent motion → no fixed shape or volume.

**Solids**
• Molecules **closely packed**; intermolecular spaces **extremely small**.
• Intermolecular forces **very strong** → positions of molecules **fixed** → definite **volume AND shape**.
• Molecules can have only **vibrational motion**.
• Cannot be compressed (close packing) → solids are **hard and rigid**; cannot flow; can be stored **without a vessel**.

**Liquids**
• Molecules **less tightly packed**; spaces somewhat more than solids.
• Forces **weak** → positions not fixed → **no shape of its own**, can **flow**.
• Forces still do not let molecules leave the liquid easily → **definite volume**; has a **definite free surface**.
• Can be **compressed only a little**; stored in a container.
• **Activity (sugar in brim-full glass):** sugar dissolves WITHOUT water spilling → liquids have **intermolecular spaces** into which sugar molecules enter.

**Gases**
• Molecules **far apart**; intermolecular space much larger than liquids.
• Forces **extremely weak / almost zero** → each molecule moves **independently** → neither definite shape nor definite volume; can be stored **only in a closed vessel**.
• Can be **compressed easily**; flows very easily; has **no free surface** of its own.
• **Activity (incense sticks/scent):** the smell spreads through the whole room — gas molecules move freely everywhere.
content.explanation:
**📊 Chart — Table 1.1: the five differences (molecular concept)**
| Property | Solids | Liquids | Gases |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packing / space | Closely packed; space extremely small | Less tight; space more than solids | No packing; space very large |
| Intermolecular force | Very strong | Weak | Almost zero |
| Position / shape / volume | Fixed positions → finite shape & size | Not fixed → no shape, finite volume | Independent → no shape, no volume |
| Motion of molecules | Vibrational only | Move inside liquid; cannot leave surface | Random translational motion |
| Compressibility | Cannot be compressed | Slightly compressible | Easily compressed |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Why can't a solid flow?
• Why does a liquid have a definite volume but not a definite shape?
• Which state has a free surface? Which has none?
• What does the sugar-in-full-glass activity prove?
• Why must a gas be kept in a CLOSED vessel?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• 'Liquids and solids are closely packed' — liquids are LESS tightly packed than solids.
• 'A gas cannot be compressed' — FALSE; gases compress most easily.
• A liquid has a definite FREE SURFACE; a gas has none.
content.examTip: Reproduce the five-row table from memory — every line of it appears somewhere as a fill-blank, T/F or match question.

content.subtopic: 3. Kinetic Theory of Matter and Energy of Molecules
content.content:
**Executive summary:** The kinetic (molecular) theory — developed about **200 years ago** by **Dalton, Joule, Davy, Maxwell, Boltzmann** and others — explains all properties of matter in terms of moving molecules. Molecular energy is **minimum in solids** (vibration only), more in liquids, **maximum in gases**; the average kinetic energy of molecules is the measure of **temperature**.

**Main points of the kinetic theory**
• Everything is composed of very small particles — **molecules**.
• Molecules of one substance are **identical in shape, size and mass**; molecules of **different** substances differ in mass, shape, size and composition.
• Molecules are **continuously in random motion**; the type of motion differs in different states.
• Molecular motion **increases with temperature** and decreases as temperature falls.
• Because of this motion, molecules possess **kinetic energy**, which **increases with temperature**.
• Molecules have **intermolecular spaces** — maximum in gases, minimum in solids.
• Molecules attract each other: **cohesion** (same substance) and **adhesion** (different substances).
• Intermolecular forces are **strongest in solids, weakest in gases**.
• **Activity (ink in water):** a drop of ink spreads until the water is uniformly coloured — molecules are in a state of motion.

**Energy of molecules in the three states**
• **Solids:** molecules can only **vibrate to and fro about their mean positions** → energy of movement is **minimum**. At low temperature the **amplitude of vibration is small**; heating increases the amplitude and kinetic energy. At the **melting point**, KE becomes sufficient to overcome intermolecular forces — molecules leave their fixed positions and the solid becomes liquid.
• **Liquids:** molecules move freely **within** the liquid but cannot leave its free surface → KE greater than in solids. On heating, KE keeps rising; at the **boiling point** the energy is enough to overcome mutual attraction — the liquid becomes gas.
• **Gases:** molecules have **independent translatory motion in all directions** through space → KE is **maximum**. The translational KE increases further on heating.
content.explanation:
**📊 Chart — Motion and energy ladder**
| State | Type of motion | Energy of molecules |
|---|---|---|
| Solid | Vibration about fixed mean positions | Minimum |
| Liquid | Free movement within the liquid (cannot leave surface) | Intermediate |
| Gas | Independent translatory motion in all directions | Maximum |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Name four scientists who developed the kinetic theory. How old is it?
• State three main points of the kinetic theory.
• What happens to the amplitude of vibration of solid molecules on heating?
• At which temperature does molecular KE first overcome intermolecular force in a solid? In a liquid?
• What does the ink-drop activity demonstrate?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• The **average kinetic energy** of molecules is a measure of the substance's **temperature**.
• 'Molecules of the whole universe are identical' — FALSE; only molecules of the SAME substance are identical.
• In liquids, molecules cannot LEAVE the surface (that needs boiling-point energy or evaporation from the surface).
content.examTip: Quote scientists' names (Dalton, Joule, Maxwell, Boltzmann) and '200 years ago' — direct fill-blank material — and keep the energy order solid < liquid < gas ready.

content.subtopic: 4. Change of State: Six Processes, Latent Heat and Kinetic Explanation
content.content:
**Executive summary:** Change of state = changing a substance from one physical state to another **by changing its temperature**. Six processes: melting/fusion, freezing/solidification, vaporisation/boiling, condensation/liquefaction, sublimation, deposition. At every change of state the **temperature stays constant** — the heat exchanged is **latent heat**, which changes the **potential energy** (separation) of molecules, not their kinetic energy.

**The six processes (all at a FIXED temperature)**
• **Melting (fusion):** solid → liquid on heating, at the **melting point**. Ice melts at **0 °C**; wax at **55 °C**. Latent heat of fusion of ice = **80 cal/g = 3.35 × 10⁵ J/kg**.
• **Freezing (solidification):** liquid → solid on cooling, at the **freezing point**. For a given substance **freezing point = melting point** (water/ice: 0 °C). Freezing point of mercury = **−39 °C**; of molten wax = **55 °C**. Water releases **80 cal/g** on freezing — latent heat of freezing equals latent heat of fusion.
• **Vaporisation (boiling):** liquid → gas on heating, at the **boiling point**. Water boils at **100 °C**; alcohol at **78 °C**. Latent heat of vaporisation of water = **540 cal/g = 2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg**.
• **Condensation (liquefaction):** gas → liquid on cooling, at the **condensation/liquefaction point**, which equals the boiling point (steam condenses at 100 °C). The substance releases exactly the heat absorbed during boiling — steam gives out 540 cal/g, which is why **steam burns are more severe than boiling-water burns**.
• **Sublimation:** solid → vapour DIRECTLY (no liquid stage), at the **sublimation point**; the solid absorbs **latent heat of sublimation**. Solids that sublime are **sublimates**; their vapour is the **sublime**. Examples: **naphthalene balls** (moth balls shrinking in the cupboard), **ammonium chloride, iodine, camphor, dry ice**.
• **Deposition (desublimation):** vapour → solid directly on cooling; heat is **released**. Examples: **snowfall** in hilly regions (cloud vapour → snowflakes directly) and **hoarfrost** on plants in extreme winter nights.

**Key activities**
• **Ice–water–steam:** ice at 0 °C stays at 0 °C while melting; the water then warms to 100 °C and stays at 100 °C while boiling — two temperature plateaus.
• **Melting point of wax / boiling point of water:** in both, the thermometer reading becomes CONSTANT when the change of state begins — that constant reading IS the melting/boiling point.
• **Ammonium chloride:** heated in a china dish under an inverted funnel (cotton plug in stem) — vapours rise (sublimation) and solidify as a thin layer on the cool funnel wall (deposition).

**Kinetic-theory explanation**
• **Melting:** heating makes solid molecules vibrate more violently; at the melting point they gain enough energy to overcome intermolecular attraction and move within the substance. The heat absorbed does NOT raise temperature — it does work against attraction to increase molecular separation.
• **Boiling:** liquid molecules' KE rises with heating; at the boiling point they overcome attraction, **leave the liquid surface** and move freely in space.
• **Sublimation:** in some solids the intermolecular forces are **comparatively weak**; heating raises molecular KE rapidly and at the sublimation point molecules break free directly. (This is also why **not all solids sublime** — most have forces too strong to skip the liquid stage.)
• Ordinarily heat raises the **average KE** (temperature rises); during change of state, heat changes the **potential energy** of molecules instead → temperature constant → hence the name **latent** ('hidden') heat.

**Dry ice:** solid carbon dioxide — looks like ice, sublimes directly to gas; used as a **refrigerant** for transporting perishable foods, meat and fish.
content.explanation:
**📊 Chart — Reference data (memorise)**
| Melting points (°C) | | Boiling points (°C) | |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ice 0 | Lead 327 | Water 100 | Ether 34.5 |
| Paraffin wax 55 | Aluminium 658 | Alcohol 78.5 | Mercury 357 |
| Naphthalene 80 | Silver 961, Copper 1083 | | |

**📊 Chart — 'Something More' facts**
| About melting point | About boiling point |
|---|---|
| Constant for a pure solid | Fixed for a pure liquid |
| No two pure solids share the same MP | No two pure liquids share the same BP |
| Changes with atmospheric pressure | INCREASES with pressure (pressure-cooker principle), decreases at low pressure |
| Changes with impurities (salt lowers ice's MP — used in making kulfi) | Changes with impurities |

**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• Name all six changes of state and the direction of each.
• Why does temperature stay constant during melting? Where does the heat go?
• Latent heat of fusion of ice and of vaporisation of water — values in cal/g and J/kg?
• Why are steam burns worse than boiling-water burns?
• Why do only SOME solids sublime? Name four sublimates.
• What is dry ice and its main use?
• How does a pressure cooker exploit the boiling point–pressure relation?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Latent heat = heat supplied/released during change of state at CONSTANT temperature.
• 'Water at 100 °C has more heat energy than steam at 100 °C' — FALSE; steam carries extra latent heat.
• 'Gas → solid directly is sublimation' — FALSE; that is DEPOSITION (sublimation is solid → gas).
• Common salt does NOT sublime.
• On a temperature–time heating graph, every FLAT (horizontal) portion is a change of state; the first plateau's temperature is the melting point.
content.examTip: Latent heat changes potential energy, never kinetic — say this line whenever a 'temperature stays constant' question appears, and quote 80 cal/g and 540 cal/g with their J/kg twins.

content.content:
Architects of the Kinetic Theory

How 200 years of physics transformed heat from a "fluid" into molecular motion

The Historical Shift: Before these scientists, heat was widely believed to be an invisible fluid called "caloric" that flowed between objects. The combined works of Dalton, Davy, Joule, Maxwell, and Boltzmann dismantled this idea, proving that matter is made of tiny particles and that heat is simply the kinetic energy of their continuous random motion.
Scientist Core Concept Contribution to Kinetic Theory
John Dalton Early 1800s Atomic Theory Provided the structural foundation. By proving that matter consists of discrete, indivisible particles (atoms/molecules), he gave the kinetic theory the physical "billiard balls" it needed to explain microscopic motion and collisions.
Humphry Davy Late 1700s / Early 1800s Heat as Motion Conducted the famous friction experiment of rubbing two pieces of ice together to melt them in a vacuum. He demonstrated that heat could be generated entirely by friction, heavily striking down the "caloric fluid" theory and proving heat is microscopic motion.
James Prescott Joule Mid 1800s Mechanical Equivalent of Heat Mathematically and experimentally proved that mechanical work directly converts into heat energy. This cemented the First Law of Thermodynamics and verified that heating a gas simply means adding kinetic energy to its molecules.
James Clerk Maxwell Mid-to-Late 1800s Statistical Averages Recognized that gas molecules do not all move at the same speed. He formulated a statistical distribution showing the range of molecular speeds at a given temperature, linking macroscopic temperature directly to average microscopic velocity.
Ludwig Boltzmann Late 1800s Statistical Mechanics Expanded Maxwell's work into the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. He mathematically linked the chaotic, random motion of individual molecules to macroscopic properties like entropy and pressure, completing the modern Kinetic Theory of Gases.
💡 Evolution of a Theory: It started with the structure (Dalton), moved to the concept of motion and energy (Davy & Joule), and was finally perfected with complex mathematics and statistics (Maxwell & Boltzmann).
